presentation.title: Distributed VLA Fine-Tuning on the Cloud: Dockerizing VLASH with DeepSpeed
presentation.subtitle: COMP4651 — Cloud Computing and Big Data Systems — HKUST

slides.title: VLASH Forge
slides.subtitle: Portable Distributed VLA Fine-Tuning on Cloud and HPC
slides.body: Distributed VLA Fine-Tuning on the Cloud: Dockerizing VLASH with DeepSpeed
slides.meta: COMP4651 — Cloud Computing and Big Data Systems — HKUST
slides.speaker_note: We built a portable, containerised distributed training platform for fine-tuning large robot learning models on cloud and HPC — deployable to Kubernetes, AWS, GCP, and HPC clusters from a single Docker image and one command.

slides.title: The Cloud Computing Problem

left_column.heading: Fine-Tuning at Scale Requires Cloud Infrastructure
bullets: 1.3B–3B parameter models — 12–40 GB GPU memory per device
Hours of distributed training across multiple GPUs
Reproducible environments across cloud VMs and HPC nodes
VLASH validated on 4×H100 — training code never released

right_column.heading: The Gap
right_column.callout: There was no open, portable distributed training infrastructure for VLASH. Anyone wanting to fine-tune had to rebuild the entire stack from scratch.
right_column.stat: 0 lines of open distributed training code from the original authors

slides.image_caption: Target deployment: Piper arm + Jetson AGX Orin
slides.speaker_note: Fine-tuning a 1.3B parameter model is a cloud computing problem — it requires 12–40 GB GPU memory, hours of distributed training, and a reproducible software stack. The VLASH framework had results but no open infrastructure.

slides.title: What We Built
slides.intro: We built the missing cloud infrastructure — three concrete contributions:

contributions.heading: Distributed Training
contributions.body: DeepSpeed ZeRO-2 and FSDP via HuggingFace Accelerate — replacing the unpublished DDP setup from the original VLASH paper

contributions.heading: Portable Containers
contributions.body: Docker + Singularity with pixi lockfile — bit-identical environments across cloud VMs, HPC nodes, and Kubernetes pods

contributions.heading: Kubernetes Deployment
contributions.body: Complete Job manifest submittable with kubectl apply — works on EKS, GKE, and AKS without modification

slides.callout: One command: ./scripts/train.sh — detects environment, launches automatically
slides.speaker_note: Three contributions: DeepSpeed distributed training, portable containers with lockfile, Kubernetes Job manifest. One command runs the whole thing.

slides.title: System Architecture
slides.callout: Nothing baked into the image — all credentials and config injected at runtime via environment variables
slides.speaker_note: Left: infrastructure flow — image pulled from Docker Hub, scheduler allocates GPU, HF Hub provides data, W&B streams metrics. Right: training pipeline inside the container.

slides.title: DeepSpeed ZeRO-2: The Core Contribution

left.label: Vanilla DDP
items: Full model replicated on every GPU

slides.callout: Enables batch_size=16 on 40GB A100s. FSDP (FULL_SHARD) available for full fine-tuning via TRAIN_BACKEND=fsdp
slides.speaker_note: ZeRO-2 shards optimizer states and gradients, 62% memory reduction. FSDP available for full fine-tuning.

slides.title: Kubernetes Deployment
slides.subtitle: Deliverable: directly deployable to a Kubernetes cluster

bullets: PersistentVolumeClaims for model cache and outputs
Works on AWS EKS, GCP GKE, Azure AKS — same manifest

right.code_snippet:
resources:
  limits:
    nvidia.com/gpu: 4
volumeMounts:
  - name: hf-cache
    mountPath: /scratch/.cache
  - name: outputs
    mountPath: /scratch/outputs
env:
  - name: HF_TOKEN
    valueFrom:
      secretKeyRef:
        name: hf-secret

slides.speaker_note: Complete Kubernetes Job manifest — GPU requests, PVCs for storage, Secret for credentials. One kubectl apply submits the full training job on any cloud provider.

slides.title: Storage Abstraction
slides.intro: The container always reads/writes to /scratch. The host bind-mounts whatever storage it has.
slides.callout: One environment variable — SCRATCH — is all that changes between environments
slides.speaker_note: Storage abstraction: /scratch is always the interface. What's behind it — Lustre, EBS, GCS, PVC — is the host's concern, not the container's.

slides.title: Results
slides.context: Training: NSCC ASPIRE 2A (4×A100 40GB) — academic HPC, no commercial cloud budget allocated. Inference: Jetson AGX Orin at 30Hz. 50 demonstrations, ~4 hours training.
slides.image_caption: π₀.₅ fine-tuned on NSCC ASPIRE, deployed on Jetson AGX Orin
slides.demo_note: See demo video — continuous 30Hz control, no chunk-boundary pauses
slides.speaker_note: 65% SR async vs 5% sync. 29.5x latency reduction. LoRA fits in 12GB per GPU confirming ZeRO-2 enables batch_size=16 across 4 A100s.

slides.title: Summary
cloud_contributions: DeepSpeed ZeRO-2 distributed training — open implementation of the missing VLASH infrastructure
Docker + Singularity containers with pixi lockfile — reproducible environments across cloud and HPC
Kubernetes Job manifest — deployable on EKS, GKE, AKS with kubectl apply
Unified /scratch storage abstraction — one env var changes between NSCC, AWS, GCP, and K8s
slides.validation: End-to-end: 65% task SR, 29.5× latency reduction on Jetson AGX Orin
future_work: Multi-node training via SLURM srun / K8s operator
slides.speaker_note: Four cloud contributions: distributed training, portable containers, K8s deployment, storage abstraction. Code, image, and docs all public.
